does: This paper addresses hallucination in large vision-language models through a data-centric approach centered on on-policy learning. The authors conduct empirical analysis showing that on-policy data substantially outperforms off-policy data for hallucination mitigation. They propose a hallucination classifier that provides clean binary annotations to avoid introducing new hallucinations during training, and develop a robust iterative direct preference optimization algorithm with dynamic sample reweighting to better leverage on-policy data. The method is evaluated on three benchmarks against eight baselines, demonstrating reduced hallucination rates.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in mitigating hallucinations in vision-language models, extending prior research on preference learning and alignment techniques like direct preference optimization (DPO). It addresses limitations in existing annotation methodologies that have been shown to inadvertently increase hallucination patterns in training data. The paper connects to broader literature on on-policy versus off-policy learning in reinforcement learning and language model fine-tuning, as well as to data quality concerns in instruction-tuning and multimodal model alignment that have become increasingly important as LVLMs scale.
enables: This work opens directions for data-efficient hallucination mitigation in vision-language models by establishing the value of on-policy training signals. The hallucination classifier approach could inspire development of task-specific cleaner annotation methods that avoid corrupting training data. The iterative DPO framework with dynamic reweighting establishes a template for future preference optimization algorithms that adapt to data quality and relevance. These findings may enable more practical deployment of LVLMs by reducing hallucinations without prohibitively expensive annotation, and point toward principled methods for controlling model behavior in multimodal reasoning tasks.
